title: New Source Review Permitting Improvement Act
short_title: New Source Review Permitting Improvement Act
stage_label: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 28 - 23.
plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for when power plants and factories need special permits from the EPA before making changes to their equipment. Under the new rules, a facility would only need a permit if the changes make it able to pollute more per hour than it could during any hour in the past 10 years. The bill also says that changes made to reduce pollution or make the facility safer or more reliable do not count as modifications that need permits. However, if the EPA thinks these safety or efficiency changes would still harm people's health or the environment, they can still require permits.
last_action_text: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 28 - 23.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the New Source Review Permitting Improvement Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Clarification of Definition of a Modification: Emission Rate Increases, Pollution Control, Efficiency, Safety, and Reliability Projects
sections.text: This section changes how we define a modification under the Clean Air Act. A modification happens when changes at a pollution source increase the amount of air pollution it creates. Under the new rules, a change only counts as increasing pollution if the highest hourly pollution rate possible after the change is higher than the highest hourly pollution rate that was possible during any hour in the ten years before the change. However, some changes do not count as modifications even if they might increase pollution. These exceptions include changes that are designed to reduce the amount of pollution created for each unit of production, or changes that are designed to restore, maintain, or improve how reliably or safely the facility operates. The only time these exceptions do not apply is when the Environmental Protection Agency determines that the increase in the maximum hourly pollution rate would cause harmful effects to human health or the environment.

sections.title: Section 3 Clarification of Definition of Construction for Prevention of Significant Deterioration
sections.text: This section changes the definition of construction for major pollution sources in areas with clean air. Construction includes modifications as defined in the previous section, but it does not include changes at major pollution facilities that do not result in a significant increase in yearly actual pollution or a significant net increase in yearly actual pollution at that facility.

sections.title: Section 4 Clarification of Definition of Modifications and Modified for Nonattainment Areas
sections.text: This section changes the definition of modifications for areas that do not meet clean air standards. The terms modifications and modified mean a modification as defined in Section Two of this law, except they do not include changes at major pollution facilities that do not result in a significant increase in yearly actual pollution or a significant net increase in yearly actual pollution at that facility.

sections.title: Section 5 Rule of Construction
sections.text: Nothing in this law should be understood to treat any change as a modification under any part of the Clean Air Act if that change would not have been treated as a modification the day before this law was passed.
